subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 7. In this unit you will master fractions – one of the most important and most useful ideas in all of mathematics. A fraction is simply a way of describing a part of a whole, or a part of a group. You already meet fractions every single day, even when you do not name them. When Amina divides a warm anjero into equal pieces to share at breakfast, when Yusuf notices that Asr is about three-quarters of the way through the afternoon, or when Halima weighs out half a kilogram of dates at the Hargeisa market, they are all thinking in fractions.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 7 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and try it yourself before you look at the answer, and only move on when a concept feels secure. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a different way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why do fractions matter so much? They are the language of sharing fairly, of measuring precisely, and of comparing quantities that do not land on whole numbers. A trader splits profit between partners using fractions. A builder in Berbera cuts a timber beam into fractional lengths. A cook scaling a recipe of bariis for twice as many guests multiplies every fractional amount. Whenever the world is divided into equal parts – and it very often is – fractions are the tool that lets you reason about it clearly.

As you work, keep a pencil and plenty of paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Redraw the bar models, write out each cancelling step, and say the rules aloud in your own words. If you make a mistake, that is not failure; it is a precise signal of exactly what to practise next. Every skill in this unit is built from a small number of clear rules, and once you understand *why* each rule works, you will rarely forget it.
Recognise proper fractions, improper fractions and mixed numbers, and explain the difference.
Find equivalent fractions by multiplying or dividing numerator and denominator by the same number.
Simplify a fraction to its lowest terms using the highest common factor.
Compare and order fractions using common denominators, cross-multiplication and decimals.
Add and subtract fractions with the same and with different denominators, including mixed numbers.
Multiply fractions, whole numbers and mixed numbers, cancelling where possible.
Divide fractions using the reciprocal (‘multiply by the flip’) method.

concepts.title: Understanding Fractions and Their Types
concepts.explanation: A fraction describes a part of a whole or a part of a group. It is written as two numbers separated by a line. The numerator on top tells you how many parts you have. The denominator on the bottom tells you how many equal parts the whole has been divided into. The word *equal* is the key – the parts must be the same size for the fraction to make sense. Picture a large sambusa platter cut into 8 equal pieces for iftar. If Hassan takes 3 pieces, he has taken 3/8 of the platter. The denominator, 8, tells us the…
concepts.example: Fractions appear everywhere once you start to look. 45 minutes is 3/4 of an hour, because an hour has 60 minutes and 45 is three of the four quarters. 25 shillings out of every 100 is 1/4 of a shilling-hundred. Winning…

concepts.title: Equivalent Fractions and Simplifying
concepts.explanation: Equivalent fractions look different but have exactly the same value. They mark the same point on the number line and describe the same amount of a whole. For example, 1/2, 2/4, 3/6, 4/8 and 5/10 are all equivalent – each one is exactly half. If you cut an anjero into 2 pieces and take 1, or cut it into 8 pieces and take 4, you have taken the same amount of bread. You create an equivalent fraction by multiplying the numerator and denominator by the same number. Because you are multiplying the top and bottom by the…
concepts.example: Going the other way, you simplify (or reduce) a fraction by dividing the numerator and denominator by the same number. A fraction is in its lowest terms when the only number that divides into both top and bottom is 1.…

concepts.title: Comparing and Ordering Fractions
concepts.explanation: To compare two fractions is to decide which is greater, which is smaller, or whether they are equal. You cannot simply look at the numbers, because a bigger denominator does not mean a bigger fraction – in fact, for the same numerator, a bigger denominator means smaller pieces. There are three reliable methods, and you should know all three. Rewrite both fractions as equivalent fractions with the same denominator, then compare the numerators. Compare 3/4 and 5/6. The LCM of 4 and 6 is 12, so use 12 as the common…
concepts.example: For fractions a/b and c/d, compare the products a × d and b × c. If a × d is larger, then a/b is larger. Compare 2/3 and 3/5: 2 × 5 = 10 and 3 × 3 = 9. Since 10 > 9, we have 2/3 > 3/5. This is really the…

concepts.title: Adding and Subtracting Fractions
concepts.explanation: You can only add or subtract fractions when the pieces are the same size – that is, when the denominators match. This is just like coins: you cannot directly add a handful of 5-shilling pieces to a handful of 10-shilling pieces until you express them in the same unit. The denominator is the ‘unit’ of a fraction, so it must be common before you combine. When the denominators are already equal, add or subtract the numerators and keep the denominator the same. The rule is a/c + b/c = (a + b)/c. For example, 2/7 + 3/7…
concepts.example: When the denominators differ, first find a common denominator – usually the LCM of the two denominators – then rewrite each fraction with that denominator, then add or subtract the numerators, and finally simplify.…

concepts.title: Multiplying Fractions
concepts.explanation: Multiplying fractions is often easier than adding them, because you do not need a common denominator. The rule is simple: multiply the numerators together, and multiply the denominators together. In symbols, a/b × c/d = (a × c)/(b × d). For example, 2/3 × 1/4 = 2/12 = 1/6, and 3/5 × 2/7 = 6/35. The word ‘of’ is the key to understanding fraction multiplication. When you work out 1/2 × 3/4 you are finding 1/2 of 3/4 – half of three-quarters. Taking a fraction of a fraction always gives a smaller result, which is why…
concepts.example: You can make the numbers smaller – and avoid heavy simplifying at the end – by cancelling common factors before multiplying. Example: 4/9 × 3/8. Here 4 and 8 share a factor of 4, so cancel them to 1 and 2; the 9 and 3…

concepts.title: Dividing Fractions
concepts.explanation: Why does flipping work? Think about the question ‘how many 1/4s are in 3/4?’ That is exactly 3/4 ÷ 1/4, and the answer is 3, because three quarter-pieces make three-quarters. Dividing tells you how many of the smaller piece fit inside the larger amount, and multiplying by the reciprocal counts them for you. The reciprocal is found by swapping numerator and denominator: the reciprocal of 3/4 is 4/3, the reciprocal of 2/5 is 5/2, and the reciprocal of a whole number 7 (that is, 7/1) is 1/7. Now work some examples:

methods.title: How to add or subtract fractions
steps: Find the LCM of the denominators.
Rewrite each fraction over that common denominator.
Add or subtract the numerators; keep the denominator.
Simplify the answer.

methods.title: How to divide fractions
steps: Convert any whole or mixed numbers to improper fractions.
Keep the first fraction as it is.
Change ÷ to × and flip the second fraction (its reciprocal).
Multiply, then simplify.

methods.title: How to find a fraction of an amount
steps: Divide the amount by the denominator.
Multiply the result by the numerator.
Check the answer is sensible (a proper fraction of an amount is smaller than the amount).

methods.title: How to convert a mixed number to an improper fraction
steps: Multiply the whole number by the denominator.
Add the numerator.
Write the result over the original denominator.

methods.title: Multiply with cancelling
steps: Solution: Cancel first.
6 and 15 share a factor of 3, becoming 2 and 5.
Then 14 and 7 share a factor of 7, becoming 2 and 1.
This leaves 2/1 × 2/5 = 4/5.

methods.title: Divide fractions
steps: Solution: Convert the mixed number: 2 1/4 = 9/4.
Check the divide fractions result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Simplify a fraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The HCF of 18 and 24 is 6. Divide both parts by 6: 18 ÷ 6 = 3 and 24 ÷ 6 = 4. So 18/24 = 3/4. Check: 3 and 4 share no factor except 1, so it is fully simplified.

workedExamples.title: Compare two fractions
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Use a common denominator. The LCM of 8 and 3 is 24. Convert: 5/8 = 15/24 and 2/3 = 16/24. Since 16 > 15, 2/3 > 5/8.

workedExamples.title: Add fractions with different denominators
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The LCM of 4 and 6 is 12. Convert: 3/4 = 9/12 and 1/6 = 2/12. Add the numerators: 9/12 + 2/12 = 11/12. It is already in lowest terms.

workedExamples.title: Subtract mixed numbers with borrowing
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The fraction part 1/4 is smaller than 3/4, so borrow one whole from the 4: 4 1/4 = 3 5/4. Now subtract: 3 5/4 − 2 3/4 = 1 2/4 = 1 1/2.

workedExamples.title: Multiply with cancelling
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Cancel first. 6 and 15 share a factor of 3, becoming 2 and 5. Then 14 and 7 share a factor of 7, becoming 2 and 1. This leaves 2/1 × 2/5 = 4/5. So the answer is 4/5.

workedExamples.title: Divide fractions
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Convert the mixed number: 2 1/4 = 9/4. Keep, change, flip: 9/4 ÷ 3/4 = 9/4 × 4/3 = 36/12 = 3.

workedExamples.title: Fraction of an amount
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Divide by the denominator, then multiply by the numerator. 32 ÷ 8 = 4, then 4 × 3 = 12.

workedExamples.title: A backwards problem
workedExamples.prompt: If 2/5 of a number is 12, what is the number?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: If 2/5 is 12, then 1/5 is 12 ÷ 2 = 6. The whole is five fifths, so 5 × 6 = 30. Check: 2/5 of 30 = 12. ✓

activities.title: Fraction Strips from Paper
activities.materials: several strips of paper the same length, a pencil, a ruler.
steps: Leave one strip whole – this is 1.
Fold a second strip into 2 equal parts and label each 1/2. Fold others into 3, 4, 6 and 8 equal parts.
Line the strips up. Which fractions are equal? (Look for 1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6 = 4/8.)
Use the strips to show that 1/2 is bigger than 1/3, even though 3 is bigger than 2. Explain why in your own words.
Keep your fraction strips – they are a model you can use all through this unit.

activities.title: Recipe Scaling in the Kitchen
activities.materials: a simple recipe (for anjero, bariis or a cake) with fractional amounts.
steps: Write down each ingredient amount as a fraction or mixed number.
Halve the recipe: multiply every amount by 1/2. Record the new amounts.
Double the recipe: multiply every amount by 2. Record these too.
Explain to a family member why 1/2 of 2 1/4 cups is 1 1/8 cups.

activities.title: The Fraction War Card Game
activities.materials: paper cards, each with a fraction written on it (mix easy and hard, such as 1/2, 2/3, 3/4, 5/8, 7/12).
steps: Shuffle the cards and deal them equally between two players.
Each player turns over one card at the same time.
Whoever has the larger fraction wins both cards – prove it using a common denominator.
Play until one player holds all the cards. Keep a note of any comparison that was hard.
This game trains you to compare fractions quickly and to justify your answer.

activities.title: Fractions of Amounts Market Stall
activities.materials: small objects to be ‘goods’ (dates, stones as shillings), and paper price labels.
steps: Set out a pile of, say, 24 dates. Ask a partner to find 3/4 of them, then 1/3, then 5/8.
Announce a ‘1/4 off’ sale: work out the new price of a 60-shilling item.
Set a backwards puzzle: ‘2/5 of my dates is 8. How many did I start with?’
Swap roles and check each other’s working.

activities.title: Design a Fraction Wall (Investigation)
activities.materials: a large sheet of paper, a ruler, coloured pencils.
steps: Draw a rectangle and split it into 1 whole, then rows of 2, 3, 4, 6, 8 and 12 equal parts.
Colour each row and label every part with its fraction.
Use the wall to find as many equivalent fractions as you can – line up the edges.
Investigate: which fractions on your wall can be simplified, and to what?

activities.title: Family Fraction Challenge
activities.materials: your family or friends, paper and pencil.
steps: Each person writes three fraction questions (one add, one multiply, one ‘fraction of an amount’).
Swap papers and solve. Show full working.
Mark each other’s answers and talk through any disagreements.
Award a point for each correct, fully simplified answer. Highest score wins.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand fractions best? What was the trickiest part – comparing, simplifying, or dividing? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Equivalent and Simplifying Multiply top and bottom by the same number to make an equivalent fraction. Divide top and bottom by their highest common factor to simplify. Lowest terms: the only common factor left is 1.

rules.title: Add and Subtract Rule
rules.text: Make the denominators the same (use the LCM), then add or subtract the numerators. Keep the common denominator; never add the denominators. Simplify the answer at the end.

rules.title: Multiply Rule
rules.text: Multiply the numerators, multiply the denominators. Cancel common factors before multiplying to keep numbers small. Convert mixed numbers to improper fractions first.

rules.title: Divide Rule
rules.text: Keep, change, flip: keep the first, change divide to multiply, flip the second. Only flip the fraction you are dividing by. Convert mixed numbers to improper fractions first.

terms: The top number – how many parts you have
The bottom number – how many equal parts in the whole
Value less than 1 / value 1 or more (top-heavy)
A whole number and a proper fraction together
Different fractions with the same value
Write a fraction in its lowest terms
Highest common factor / lowest common multiple
commonMistakes: Flipping the first fraction when dividing
Flip only the divisor: a/b ÷ c/d = a/b × d/c

realProblems.prompt: Amina walked 3/8 of a kilometre to the masjid and 1/4 of a kilometre home a different way. How far did she walk altogether?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A baker has 3 1/2 kilograms of flour. She uses 1 3/4 kg for bread and 7/8 kg for cookies. How much flour is left? Give a mixed number.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A ribbon 7 1/2 metres long is cut into pieces each 1 1/4 metres long. How many pieces are there?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Sarah spent 2/5 of her money on books and 1/4 on clothes. If she spent 35 shillings on books, how much did she start with, and how much did she spend on clothes?
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: A recipe for 6 people needs 2 1/4 cups of rice. How much rice is needed for 8 people?
realProblems.answer: Started with 87.50 shillings (35 ÷ 2/5); clothes cost 1/4 × 87.50 = 21.88 shillings (to the nearest cent).
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

questions.question: Work out 6/7 × 14/15.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 7 methods.
questions.explanation: Cancel first. 6 and 15 share a factor of 3, becoming 2 and 5. Then 14 and 7 share a factor of 7, becoming 2 and 1. This leaves 2/1 × 2/5 = 4/5. So the answer is 4/5.

questions.question: What does “Numerator” mean?
options: The top number – how many parts you have
The bottom number – how many equal parts in the whole
Value less than 1 / value 1 or more (top-heavy)
A whole number and a proper fraction together
questions.answer: The top number – how many parts you have
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Numerator means The top number – how many parts you have.

options: The bottom number – how many equal parts in the whole

A whole number and a proper fraction together
Different fractions with the same value

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, copy every worked example onto paper and try it yourself before you look at the answer, and only move on when a concept feels secure. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a different way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why do fractions matter so much? They are the language of sharing fairly, of measuring precisely, and of comparing quantities that do not land on whole numbers. A trader splits profit between partners using fractions. A builder in Berbera cuts a timber beam into fractional lengths. A cook scaling a recipe of bariis for twice as many guests multiplies every fractional amount. Whenever the world is divided into equal parts – and it very often is – fractions are the tool that lets you reason about it clearly.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and plenty of paper beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading – you must do it. Redraw the bar models, write out each cancelling step, and say the rules aloud in your own words. If you make a mistake, that is not failure; it is a precise signal of exactly what to practise next. Every skill in this unit is built from a small number of clear rules, and once you understand *why* each rule works, you will rarely forget it.
I can recognise proper fractions, improper fractions and mixed numbers, and explain the difference.
I can find equivalent fractions by multiplying or dividing numerator and denominator by the same number.
I can simplify a fraction to its lowest terms using the highest common factor.
I can compare and order fractions using common denominators, cross-multiplication and decimals.
I can add and subtract fractions with the same and with different denominators, including mixed numbers.
